data.title: TTM Technologies: The AI Bottleneck Story Is Fully Valued, Earnings Preview (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I Think TTMI Has Become Fairly Valued, With Capped Upside

TTM Technologies, Inc. (TTMI) is up over 150% over the past 6 months, beating most names not just in the tech sector, but also in the hottest niches of these days. I wrote about the stock twice, and the most recent article that I published in mid-December 2025 seems to have aged pretty well:

I like the general business prospects and the level of execution at TTMI right now - fundamentally, there are no clear red flags that could have led me to a downgrade. However, there's a lot of optimism already priced in. TTMI's key valuation multiples have expanded significantly since the beginning of this year; even if we assume there should be an additional premium on the current consensus to reflect likely earnings revisions or beats ahead, the stock looks quite expensive regardless. Therefore, my main finding this time is that TTM Technologies won't offer newcomers a sufficient level of upside potential unless some overly bullish catalysts begin to play out. Given all this, I'm downgrading TTMI to "Hold" this time ahead of the upcoming earnings release (April 29, 2026, per Seeking Alpha).

Why Do I Think So?

It's hard to believe TTMI was trading at just $20-25/share in 2025, and now it's at almost $150. The market's narrative has changed, and it's not reasonless. For years, the market viewed TTMI as if it were just a commodity-like PCB (printed circuit board) and EMC (electronic manufacturing services) provider; in reality, however, the firm proved to be a crucial infrastructure provider for key AI trends. Something similar was seen in the memory market - take a look at Micron (MU) or its South Korean rivals (Samsung and SK Hynix) and their price action for the past 24 months.

The bottleneck that TTMI solves involves HDI (high-density interconnect) and HLC (high-layer count) boards, which often stack 40 to 60 layers, and will soon stack more >100, ultimately allowing AI models to run effectively. There are only a few companies globally capable of manufacturing these specialized boards at scale, and TTMI is one of them. Plus, they're headquartered in Santa Ana, California, so it's a go-to solution for all US investors looking for exposure to this narrow niche. In addition, TTMI is a trusted foundry for the U.S. Department of Defense when it comes to critical RF (radio frequency) components, so the actual TAM, amid news of increased defense spending, has grown dramatically. The narrative change is real, and it's not groundless at all, in my understanding.

The narrative boost has also been supported by real fundamentals. In Q4, we saw TTMI reporting $774.3 million in sales (+19% YoY) and adjusted EPS of $0.70 (+16.66% YoY) - both figures beat the consensus estimates by 2.84% and 2.56%, respectively, per Seeking Alpha. The main driver for the top line's expansion is, as you might have guessed, their data center segment, where sales have managed to grow by 57% YoY in Q4 alone. The Aerospace and Defense segment grew by just 4% YoY in the quarter, but TTMI takes in orders 46% faster than it can fulfill them, so the earnings visibility remains strong. Plus, the share of the A&D contribution to the consolidated mix is gradually falling, so we might see a re-acceleration in growth, just like we've seen with AMD (AMD) last year.

Despite the headline figures' strength, there's also the other side of the coin. The company's operating cash flows declined by almost a third in Q4 on a YoY basis, and as a result, the FCF fell by 64% to just $11.7 million. For a $16+ billion market cap company, it's actually ugly news. CAPEX consumed the largest portion of what could have been left for FCF - TTMI spent ~$273.9 million in CapEx in 2025 (over 9% of sales) to prepare its high-tech facilities in Penang, Malaysia, and Syracuse, New York - so it appears to be a necessary expenditure for its future growth. But in this case, what about the OCF generation (CAPEX aside)?

Anyway, I actually like what they've been spending on. For example, their facility in NY (Syracuse) was specifically targeting Ultra-HDI tech for defense and commercial applications, and the management stated that they were targeting the first revenues from this facility to come in 2H 2026. So, the latter half of FY2026 should already show some contribution to the top line, and, presumably, the bottom line should also feel the bullish impact (assuming the operating leverage holds). Another example - their Penang facility - I view it as a non-China alternative for high-end PCB manufacturing. It has already doubled its revenues in Q4 2025, and I assume that growth will continue into 2H 2026 and beyond. It is pressuring margins to date, but the compression effect is likely to fade with economies of scale through fiscal 2026.

Another bullish catalyst for TTMI comes from its recent acquisition of a massive 750,000-square-foot facility in Wisconsin, late last year, which is going to be ready over the next 18 to 24 months. Once done, it'll add to their supply capabilities well beyond 2026-2027. We see major US hyperscalers trying to pivot away from Chinese PCB vendors, with Amazon (AMZN) expecting to increase its ASIC PCB share with TTM to over 15% in 2026, and Meta Platforms (META) likely willing to handle TTM a major share of its ASIC projects. So, once the Wisconsin-based capacity is ready to ship, I think it's likely to support the longevity of TTMI's superior top-line growth ahead. The consensus data generally agrees on growth ahead, but I believe the actual figures for FY2027-2028 are still a bit underestimated:

For the upcoming earnings release, the management guided for $770-810 million in sales and non-GAAP EPS of $0.64-0.70, which equates to $790 million and $0.67 at the midpoints, respectively. The market expects the same EPS, but I see a slight underestimation when it comes to sales:

This fact definitely increases the odds of another beating.

Investors should watch TTMI's data center and networking sales (these end markets have been merged into a single reporting segment) - the management expects it to represent a massive 37% of total Q1 sales, if my math is right. The combination of such hyper-growth end markets into a single category will likely be a supporting factor for the non-A&D part of TTMI's business mix throughtout the whole 2026 because both individual segments are currently exhibiting explosive growth, and any sudden setback in one of them will likely be supported by the other. At least it's my view on the matter.

Anyway, another thing that interests me about the upcoming release is the guidance updates on the Syracuse Ultra-HDI facility, which should scale in late 2026, and also the giant Eau Claire facility that should come online in late 2027. If the management doubles down on its previous target and timeline, I think it's going to be a positive catalyst for the stock. By the end of 2026, the management expected the CAPEX to peak, so the FCF should increase back to its previous levels and beyond them over time - we need positive comments on that possibility as well.

What I don't like as we're approaching the Q1 print is TTMI's multiple expansion and the premium priced-in. For years, the average P/E for TTMI has been in the range of 25-35x, and now, the stock's forward P/E is over 46x, according to YCharts.

The FWD ratio drops to ~33x by the end of FY2027, according to Seeking Alpha, and the implied PEG ratio should be close to 0.80x, which makes TTMI look undervalued when adjusted for prospective earnings expansion.

However, the upside looks capped if we assume that TTMI's FY2027 P/E doesn't move from the current 30-35x range. Assuming a 5% premium on top of today's FY2027 EPS, and taking the range I've just suggested, I get a price target of $155-156/share. This is only 3-4% above today's stock price.

The Bottom Line

While I still like the business, the localized focus of the PCB production in the future years, and TTMI's market positioning in its key niches, I don't like the valuation after the massive rally of the past months. The upcoming earnings report might easily make TTMI an overvalued story if it rips by 20-25% on the earnings release as a bullish catalyst (we see this kind of price action quite often in popular so-called "tech bottleneck names" these days). Anyway, the lack of a reasonable upside on the normalized P/E multiple, even with some premium for superior business growth, leads me to a downgrade (from "Buy" to "Hold").

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Intel: The Unlikely Turnaround Story
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Go Finance Intelligence as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Intel Corporation (INTC) just released its fiscal Q1 2026 results, and it is now evident that progress is being made in the foundry segment. To me, this is interesting because it is a testament to improved operational execution. Significant growth is driven by 18A wafers, which are now in full-volume production ramp. Let’s see something here. Out of the $5.4 billion in revenue generated from the foundry, $174 million came from the external foundry business. It seems modest, but when I look at it through the lens of Intel’s 10-K, which stated that no solid external customers had been recorded at the time, the $174 million makes a good start.

You see, while internal foundry revenues are picking up strongly, external foundries are also showing the same trend through partnerships, particularly the ones with Google and Tesla. I will delve into this later, but to mention, the partnership with Google involves the production of Xeon 6 processors for AI purposes. Also, isn’t it interesting that Intel just landed Tesla as one of its high-profile customers for its 14A technology? Honestly, I think this is because, contrary to the fears highlighted in 10-K around 14A uncertainty, this now removes that uncertainty with Tesla as one certain customer.

As such, I am rating Intel as a “Buy.” Let’s now get into the details to unearth why I believe this company deserves a bullish rating.

A Deep Dive Into Partnerships

My view on Intel is long-term bullish, given its commitments, such as partnerships, particularly with Google. Even though Intel’s foundry remains a work in progress, these partnerships are the gateway to securing foundry customer certainty. Google’s partnership is a good example because it involves the sale of the latest Intel Xeon 6 processors targeted for AI workloads and will be built on Intel’s 18A process nodes. Note that Google plans to double its expenditure in 2026 to $185 billion from $91.4 billion in 2025, and this might be Intel’s opportunity to make a cut with its Xeon 6 processors. Looking back at Q1 2026, the design commitments are expected to begin between 2026 and 2027.

In fact, when I look at the partnership between Tesla and Intel, where Tesla’s $20 billion TeraFab is planning to use 14A chip manufacturing processes, it signals that Intel’s foundry business could be entering an inflection point. Well, Intel has not yet provided its current backlog, but hints of customer prepayments by EMIB-T and EMIB give me optimism about this segment.

In other words, Intel is consolidating its fundamentals for future foundry market leadership. For example, Intel recently began mass production of the 18A process in its Fab 52 facility, which could challenge its competitors in the production of 2-nanometer (nm) chips. With potential customers such as Google through partnerships, I believe Intel has upside potential. Right now, from my perspective, it is interesting to see that Intel’s 2nm chip is the most advanced chip produced in the U.S., positioning it to compete with TSM. To me, these partnerships are an entry through which Intel is consolidating its customer base and a reason to be optimistic in the long term.

My Thoughts on Foundry Business: Optimism Beyond Q1-26

The semiconductor sector is increasingly promising, with its market volume expected to hit $1 trillion by 2027. This will be driven by the AI chips, which are expected to reach a market volume of $500 billion by the end of 2026, which is basically around half of the chip sales. It indicates that the foundry market is still in its early stages and has a high potential for growth.

Intel looks forward to meeting the demand for advanced packaging services by 2027, when its backlog is expected to convert to revenues. It indicates that Intel is positioning itself to access this rapidly growing sector, which is likely to reach a trillion by 2027. For example, Intel is in computing and data storage, which are major drivers of the semiconductor market revenues in 2026. 18A is used to make these computing and data storage and memory ICs. Since wafer demand has been outpacing supply, I believe the Q1 2026 update on manufacturing expansion reveals Intel’s preparedness to meet these demands. I see this as an important update because it shows that Intel is committed to controlling about 20% of the global advanced logic market share by the end of 2026. Following the conclusion of the CHIPS Act by the Trump Administration and the U.S. holding about a 10% stake in Intel, I believe this gives the company an upper hand in winning U.S. defense and classified workloads.

Also, most importantly, I do not see any major concerns with the FY2025 reported impairment and depreciation of its manufacturing machines, resulting in a non-cash expense of $950 million. Even the $3.9 billion goodwill impairment recognized in Q1 2026 as a non-cash expense is related to Mobileye, not to Intel’s foundry. Specifically, let me talk about the $950 million impairment. I am looking at it in two. One is that it is primarily related to Intel’s 7 process node, a legacy deep ultraviolet-generation manufacturing technology that is now in surplus. My second reason is that Intel is using the world’s first High-NA EUV lithography to manufacture its 14A and 18A with an output capacity of 175 wafers per hour. It is also rumored that Intel is raising its orders for NA EUV machines and that in 2025, it received $5 billion in external funding from NVDA and $2 billion from SoftBank. To me, this is worthwhile progress, indicating that Intel has a financial edge to turn the unprofitable foundry into a profitable operation in the near future. Let’s now delve into Q1 '26 financial highlights to unravel another picture of why I believe Intel deserves a bullish rating.

Q1 2026 Financial Highlights

Intel has started FY2026 on a high note, with the overall top line reaching $13.5 billion, up 7.18% YoY. This was Intel’s fourth consecutive quarter in which it beat analysts’ revenue estimates by 9.22%. It is worth noting that other Intel segments are doing well, such as Client Computing Group [CCG], which is $7.7 billion, up 1% YoY, and Data Center and AI (DCAI), $5.1 billion, up 22% YoY. For the sake of this article, Intel’s foundry revenues grew 16% YoY to $5.4 billion, showing that the segment, which has been dragging the company’s financial performance, is catching up gradually. The foundry segment’s operating margin, which had bottomed out at -71% and is now trailing at 45.0%, gives this rebound signal.

Looking ahead to the next quarter, I believe that, given my optimistic outlook for the Foundry segment, Intel is likely to beat analysts’ revenue estimates, hitting $13.6 billion, representing 11.80% YoY growth. From a trend analysis angle, it seems that Intel’s second-quarter trends have been following the first quarter’s trajectory by recording a higher revenue score in the last four years. Other segments are also expected to continue doing well. For example, the company is committed to meeting the needs of all its customers, and as such, the CCG segment and DCAI supply are expected to improve, with DCAI revenues expected to double. With this fundamentally driven Q2 '26 outlook, the trend picture is likely to happen and beat analyst estimates.

From an earnings perspective, my optimism is not far from analysts’ earnings estimates. In fiscal Q2 '26, Intel could likely beat analysts’ EPS estimates of $0.21 after missing in Q1 '26 by 306.37% to -$0.73. It shows that Intel, driven by improvements in its foundry, is heading towards profitability. In Q1 '26, the net loss deepened to -$3.7 billion, up from -$591 million, which explains why the EPS score went down significantly. This is likely to be driven by expanded manufacturing facilities such as Arizona Fab 52, which has the capacity for a mass production ramp.

Valuation

I will compare Intel’s trailing P/S of 5.72x to those of its peers, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). AMD’s P/S is trailing at 14.31x and TSM at 13.29x, which is a relatively modest distance from Intel’s 5.72x. From the surface, the current P/S might seem like Intel is trading at a market price, but I think it is trading at a discount from a growth perspective. In a broader perspective, using forward revenue growth highlights the discounted price more clearly.

Intel’s forward revenue growth stands at 3.58% at a P/S of 5.72x, which is distant from AMD’s 38.16% at 14.31x P/S and TSM's 32.88% at a P/S of 13.29x. From this picture, Intel’s current P/S reflects its current sales and its fundamentals, pointing to growth expectations driven by the semiconductor sector. At a forward revenue growth rate of 3.58%, I think Intel’s current price is discounted. As such, I believe this is a perfect entry point, which is why I am bullish.

What Might Go Wrong

Persistent losses remain a major risk at Intel, driven by the foundry segment. Despite the segment recording $4.5 billion in revenue, the operating loss still reached -$2.4 billion, resulting in an operating margin of -45.0%. This is progress from Q4 2025’s operating margin of -71.7%, but as stated above, this remains a work-in-progress segment whose potential has not translated into financial sustainability. In the event of unforeseen challenges in the semiconductor sector, this could prolong the period of unprofitability.

Takeaway

I am reiterating my bullish stance on Intel, driven by the improving foundry segment. I believe this segment has significant long-term potential that should not be overlooked, given its rapid growth in the semiconductor sector.

data.title: Paccar's Rough Ride Warrants Caution As Earnings Near
data.description:
I have historically done quite well in the market. But the market does not always agree with me, especially in the short run. A good example of this can be seen by looking at my decision in August of last year to downgrade shares of PACCAR (PCAR) from a ‘hold’ to a soft ‘sell.’ Even though I acknowledged that it is a solid business, I had become increasingly worried about deteriorating market conditions and the prospect that a recession that I still believe is coming will negatively affect truck sales volumes and profitability. We have actually seen some weakness for the company since then. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all taken a hit. And yet, shares of the business have skyrocketed 29.9%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up just 10%.

Some of the optimism likely stems from new data that management came out with regarding the market opportunity that exists for the company and some of the ways it is focusing on achieving growth. Long term, I have no doubt that the company will do just fine. But as things stand, shares are rather pricey on an absolute basis, even if they might be on the cheaper end of the spectrum in relation to other similar firms. Because of this, I cannot bring myself to upgrade the company at this time.

It is always possible, of course, that my opinion could change as new data comes in. It just so happens that, on April 28th, management will be announcing results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will take a hit, though profitability is forecasted to improve. But unless we see something significant change, I don't see myself upgrading the company at this time.

Taking PACCAR for a test drive

Operationally speaking, PACCAR is an interesting business. Management describes the company as a multinational firm that is really broken up into three different industry segments. The largest of these by far is the Truck segment, which involves the design, production, and distribution of light, medium, and heavy duty commercial trucks. We also have the Parts segment, which includes the distribution of aftermarket parts for trucks and related commercial vehicles. And then finally, we have the smallest segment, but certainly an incredibly important one from a profit standpoint. And that happens to be the Financial Services segment, which involves finance and leasing products and services that are provided to customers and dealers. These are mostly focused on the firm's own products and related equipment.

As of this moment, the newest data that we have for PACCAR covers through the end of the 2025 fiscal year. Compared to 2024, it was definitely a letdown. Revenue for that time came in at $28.44 billion. That was down from the $33.66 billion reported for 2024 and the $35.13 billion reported for 2023. A lot of the weakness in revenue for the company has come from its Truck segment. After the COVID-19 pandemic, there was an initial surge of activity caused by supply chain shortages. This caused a massive increase in investment in commercial trucks. Unfortunately, however, that surge came to bite the industry back. I say that because when industry conditions encourage a front-loading of capacity, eventually, you see significant weakening. Add on top of this worsening economic conditions caused by high-interest rates and inflationary pressures, the latter of which is being driven in large part now by tariffs, and it's not shocking to see demand for transportation decline.

From 2023 through 2025, the number of units shipped by the Truck segment fell from 204,200 to just 144,200. That pushed revenue for the segment down from $26.85 billion to $19.37 billion. Pre-tax profits, meanwhile, plummeted from $3.80 billion to $870.8 million. This was instrumental in pushing PACCAR’s overall profits lower, as the first chart in this article illustrates. This includes not only net income but also operating cash flow, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA.

Now this doesn't mean that the future for the business won't be bright. Across all of its operating segments, the firm has invested more than $5 billion over the last five years. This has included not only expanding capacity when it comes to legacy platforms but also investing more heavily in new technologies for the future. One example would be its autonomous truck platform. Management even boasts its L4 autonomy technology, with more than 150 weekly autonomous trips taking place. The firm is even planning initial commercial deployments of this technology. This is a huge opportunity for the business. Management believes that value creation could be around $25 billion on an annual basis. This would come from a significant reduction in personnel costs. There are other examples as well. For instance, the company’s XD XF Electric truck is an emissions-friendly vehicle that was awarded the International Truck of the Year award.

This is not to say that all parts of the company performed poorly in the short run. The Parts segment has been surprisingly resilient. Over the last three years, revenue rose from $6.41 billion to $6.87 billion. Meanwhile, pre-tax profits dipped only slightly from $1.70 billion to $1.67 billion. But the overall growth potential that exists here is significant.

Management claims to have a 15% market share in the North American parts industry. The total size of that space is worth about $45 billion. And in Europe, it has a 13% market share of the $25 billion parts market. By 2030, the firm is hoping to capture an additional 5% stake in this expanding market. That would boost revenue by $3.5 billion. It actually makes a lot of sense that the Parts market would be doing so well right now.

According to management, for a first owner, for the first four years that a truck is owned, you're looking at about $8,000 in annual spending per truck. And for second owners, that number is about $12,000 per year for years 5 through 12. Right now, because of how difficult industry conditions are, it's a lot cheaper to invest in fixing up existing vehicles than it is to buy new ones. So if anything, this downturn is a source of relative strength for the segment.

And surprisingly, management has done a fine job when it comes to the Financial Services portion of the business. Despite overall revenue having dropped, segment revenue here increased from $1.81 billion to $2.21 billion. Management chalked this expansion up primarily to the capture of additional market share in the regions of the world in which it operates.

This is fantastic, and it demonstrates the overall quality of the company. This is particularly important considering just how profitable this segment is. It accounts for just 7.8% of revenue between the top three operating segments of the company. And yet, it is responsible for 16.1% of those pre-tax profits.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using results from both 2025 and 2024. I wish that I could provide an estimate when it comes to 2026. Management did say that they expect overall revenue to expand by between 4% and 8%. But they have not given enough detail to arrive at a good and reliable estimate for the bottom line. Having said that, even though the stock isn't priced all that poorly based on estimates from 2024, it is a bit lofty when it comes to the 2025 figures. Relative to other similar firms, it's closer to the middle of the group. In the aforementioned chart, you can see that two of the five firms are cheaper than it is on both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it is more expensive than four of the five companies I compared it to.

At the start of this article, I made clear that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of PACCAR's 2026 fiscal year on April 28th. As the table above illustrates, analysts believe that revenue will decline but that profitability will rise. Management has been working on cost-cutting opportunities, which likely explains this optimism. But unless the firm can come out with really stellar results, I don't see myself upgrading the business.

Takeaway

The only reason why I am not neutral on the business right now is because I do worry about the economy more broadly. As I have detailed in other articles, like here and here, I believe that we are heading toward a recession. That will naturally lead to some weakness in this industry. We are already seeing softness in the freight transportation market. According to one source, for instance, freight demand has softened, though it is expected that spot rates for truckload freight could edge up modestly this year compared to last year. Unfortunately, operational costs remain high, and this latest war that the US has started against Iran is not doing the market any favors because of increased fuel costs. There also exists a shortage of drivers, which could accelerate automation, but which ultimately means that the ability to transport goods is more limited, which would necessarily translate to weaker demand. Until we see some signs of improvement in the space, I cannot in good faith upgrade the company at this time.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 9.5% (April 2026)
data.description:
Introduction

We have had a roller-coaster ride in the last couple of months. Due to the events in the Middle East and their impact on energy prices, the stock market took a nosedive for a couple of weeks. However, the recovery has been very quick, and the market has surpassed even the previous highs. Even though the peace in the Middle East is still elusive and oil prices are still very high than they were two months ago, the S&P 500 is trading comfortably above the SPX7000 level. However, the question is whether this is sustainable. A lot depends on how the events turn out in the near term and on the energy prices in the short to medium term. How the Fed acts in the coming months will be interesting to watch.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch Tool website.

All that being said, buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.10%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally very stressful and full of pitfalls, and that's why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI Stocks.' You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance (at least on a relative basis), and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 9.6% (as of 04/17/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 16.0%, 8.8%, and 9.9% (annualized) in the last three, five, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.7%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why is It Important to Have a Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization > 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we were generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down to 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top ten.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 04/17/2026)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (Last Three Years)

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions vs. Earnings)

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds, as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list by coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 31 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: DPG, EDF, EIC, EMO, GNT, GOF, KYN, and SCD (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: AIO, BGR, CSQ, EOS, GAM, GGN, NXG, PEO, TWN, and XFLT (20 duplicates).

Appeared four times: PDX (3 duplicates).

Once we remove 31 duplicate rows, we are left with 44 (75-31) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors, like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 44 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_APR_2026.xlsx

10 Positions Portfolio of the Month

Before we get to the final ten, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 10 list, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the List of the Top 20 Selections (From Different Asset Classes) in the Order of Sector:

Table 8:

10 Best CEFs of the Month

In the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the List of the Top 10 Selections This Month (In Order of Sector):

(EOS), (TEI), (ETG), (EIC), (PTY), (RNP), (BANX), (NXG), (AIO), and (GAM).

Table-9:

Some Information About the Selections

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on many of our selections:

Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS)

In our diversified list of 10 funds, we usually select one fund that is based on covered calls. The Eaton Vance fund, EOS, often makes it to our list as it is one of the best funds in the 'covered calls' category. Its high exposure to the technology sector has definitely helped the fund to shine in the last 10 years. It currently holds only about 50 companies and overwrites (sells options) on roughly 50% of its portfolio to generate income and reduce volatility. The distribution yield is attractive at 8.3% and currently offers a decent discount of -9.1%. The fund uses no leverage, and its expenses are comparable to those of its competitors. The past performance record is solid, and it has returned 18%, 9%, and 14% (annualized) over the last 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (TEI)

This is a fixed-income fund invested in emerging market economies around the world. It is primarily invested in income-producing securities of sovereign (or sovereign-related) entities as well as private sector companies in the emerging market countries. Since it involves emerging markets, the risk profile is higher compared to a similar fund invested in developed markets. That said, it has provided a reliable monthly income in the last several years. Prior to 2018, the distribution frequency was on a quarterly basis. The current yield is attractive at 8.5% plus, and the discount is roughly -6%. The distribution appears to be fully covered by the investment income. Funds like this can provide high income, but the capital appreciation is not their strong point. However, buying them during out-of-favor cycles can also provide some capital appreciation.

Eaton Vance Tax-Advtgd Global Dividend Income Fund (ETG)

As the name suggests, the fund is offered by the Eaton Vance group. It invests primarily in equities and, to a smaller extent, preferred securities of dividend-paying companies from around the world. Nearly 45% of the assets are invested in U.S. companies, and the rest is invested in Europe, the U.K., Canada, Japan, and some emerging economies. Over 85% of the assets are in equities, and the rest are in bonds, preferred securities, and cash. The primary goal of the fund is to provide tax-advantaged income in the form of dividends and long-term capital gains. The current yield is slightly on the lower side at 7.1%, but the discount is attractive at -10.5%.

Eagle Point Income Company Inc (EIC)

EIC is probably the riskiest fund in this selection of 10 funds. The fund uses high leverage of roughly 35% in the form of preferred stock. The fund primarily invests in junior debt tranches of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). In addition, it may invest up to 35% of total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities.

Due to the decline in interest rates earlier, similar to its peers, the fund has been reducing its distribution amount. The monthly distribution has come down from $0.20 a share to $0.10, a 50% reduction. This has caused the price to fall during most of 2025, and that has continued in 2026, but the pace has slowed down. So, it has been brutal for someone who acquired the shares prior to 2024. However, the yield is still very attractive for the new buyers at nearly 13.5%, in spite of the lower distribution amount. The discount has widened to very high levels (currently at -17%), though they have narrowed somewhat in the last month. On the positive side, with reduced distribution, the current distribution is fully covered by the NII (net investment income). We think that after significant correction, the price and yield are attractive.

PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY)

PTY is a multi-sector fund offered by the PIMCO group. Usually, the fund trades at a high premium, but recently the premium has narrowed quite a bit. The current premium of 4.95% is relatively low compared to 14%+ for the 52-week average and 21% for the 5-year average. We think this offers a good entry point.

PTY is mostly a multi-sector bond fund with global exposure, with nearly 30%-40% exposure to international markets. The fund invests in a diversified mix of income-producing assets and focuses on generating and providing its investors with a steady stream of income. The fund is down because of the headwinds in the bond market due to the declining interest rates. But more recently, expectations for the interest rates have changed to either staying flat or even rising. Moreover, the fund has a history of maintaining its NAV, in spite of high income, currently at 11.7%.

Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred & Income Fund Inc (RNP)

This is a hybrid fund and provides nearly identical exposure to real estate equity, mostly REITs, and the other half to preferred and fixed income securities. The fund also uses significant leverage of over 30%. The real estate sector had a tough few years due to the elevated interest rates. But interest rates have come down significantly from their highs in 2024, and that has helped the sector recover somewhat in 2025 and 2026. Though the future direction of interest rates is far from clear, most likely they are not going to go up. The fund currently offers a moderate discount of roughly -4.5% and a decent distribution yield of 7.76%.

ArrowMark Financial Corp (BANX)

The fund's primary objectives are to provide a high and stable income to its investors while maintaining a stable NAV. The fund invests in subordinated debt, convertible securities, regulatory capital relief securities, preferred equity, and, to a lesser extent, common equity.

The fund currently offers a reasonably high yield of 9.1%, which is fully covered and supported by the NII (net investment income). It is also trading at a decent discount of -6%. It offers exposure to floating-rate instruments that have benefited from the rise in interest rates in recent years; however, that may appear to be a negative right now. But rates may not decline any further in a hurry, and certainly, we are not going to see zero interest rates any time soon. Moreover, the management is quick to adapt to changing conditions and ensure that the distribution is fully covered and the NAV is maintained.

NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG)

NXG was originally launched in 2012 but has undergone rebranding a couple of times. It has been in its current form and structure since 2022. The fund invests in equities of infrastructure and energy companies. It also uses significant leverage to the extent of roughly 30%. The major invested sectors include energy, industrial, technology, communications, and sustainable energy infrastructure. The fund has benefited from the expected surge in energy demands from the AI data centers, and the trend is likely to continue. It pays a high level of yield, roughly over 12%; however, please note that a large portion of the distribution amount is paid as return of capital (which is considered tax-friendly). This should not worry investors as long as the NAV is stable or growing. Recently, the discount has widened to nearly 8% due to the ongoing rights offering, which is closing on April 30th. So, it offers a good entry point as well.

Virtus Artificial Intelligence & Technology Opp Fd (AIO)

This is a fund focused on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and technology companies and is a good fit for a diversified income-producing portfolio. The fund comes with a unique flavor of combining both equity and convertible securities, as well as some bonds, based on market conditions. The fund also uses modest leverage of 10%–15% and has performed very well in the last three years. The current distribution yield is nice at 7.5% plus and is currently trading at an attractive discount of roughly -11%.

General American Investors Company Inc (GAM)

For the core U.S. equity segment, GAM or Adams Diversified Equity Fund, Inc. (ADX) is a good pick. We select GAM this month as ADX appears to be more expensive. However, please note that GAM does not provide quarterly distributions, but only an annual distribution, which is at a variable rate. The income investors who like to have regular quarterly (or monthly) distributions should avoid GAM. Based on the distribution paid in December 2025, the yield is quite attractive at close to 10%. The current discount is -10.5%.

The primary investment objective of the fund is to seek capital appreciation, with income only a secondary objective. The fund invests in equity securities with growth potential at reasonable valuations. The fund also uses a modest leverage of roughly 10% in the form of preferred stock.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.10%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of 20 CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 9.6% (as of 04/17/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 16.0%, 8.8%, and 9.9% (annualized) in the last three, five, and ten years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.7%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

data.title: EOG Resources: American Oil Producer Pumping Excess Shareholder Rewards
data.description:
Yesterday, we covered what we believe to be one of the best current commodity-focused investments based on a limited view of oil price volatility. Our general perspective is that oil price volatility will continue so long as the Iran war continues, but once the Iran war concludes, oil production and transportation will recover to predicted levels. We expect oil prices to fall back towards the $50-$60 range again, assuming that there is no wide-scale oil production infrastructure damage caused during the war. We use this as our worst-case expectation for oil prices throughout 2026. With this forecast, we are focused on commodity investments that will provide strong rewards to shareholders due to increased free cash flow from a limited period of oil price volatility. When we covered Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), we highlighted the fact that their low breakeven point per barrel and structure that's clearly designed to reward shareholders from higher free cash flow mean that as long as the war persists, they will be able to provide excess cash flow-based rewards to shareholders.

Today, I want to shift our focus from Canada back to the United States of America and look at an oil and gas producer within the United States that is structured similarly.

Let's dive in!

American Oil Producer With Eyes on Shareholder Returns

EOG Resources (EOG) is an American-based oil and gas producer. We last covered EOG on March 19.

While EOG is an American-based oil and gas producer, it does have oil and gas holdings within the Caribbean as well as the Middle East. However, the vast majority of their production comes from safe domestic production locations that are not exposed to any sort of military action or risk of military action. Furthermore, EOG is focused on having a low break-even point, similar to how we highlighted yesterday within Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) that a low break-even point can mean that any price above that can become pure free cash flow or profit for the company and shareholder returns if that is the focus of the company.

In their last earnings call, EOG highlighted that to cover their entire planned capital spending program and regular dividends to shareholders, they had a breakeven price of $50 using West Texas Intermediate (WTI).

Currently, WTI is well above this breakeven point. This means their current plan is well in hand for current shareholders.

EOG had a wonderful beginning of the year but has cooled off as hopes that the Iran war is coming to a close and oil prices will settle back down grow. It's interesting to see that it was bid higher, just like we saw with CNQ, and only recently started to be sold off again. Is the Iran war ending? No one really knows, as both sides continue to claim victory in various aspects. In the end, we aren't attempting to predict the conclusion point, only to find returns from the volatility created in oil prices.

So was EOG overpriced? Not in our opinion, no.

Using EOG's 10-year operating cash flow (OCF) to price ratio, we can see that they have typically traded around 7.6x. This is our current evaluation measure that we use for EOG to determine whether it's overpriced or not, and that's the blue line on this chart. Currently, EOG is well below that value right now, but it did climb up close to that when it was at its high earlier in March. OCF is expected to jump strongly because of higher oil prices, and that will lead to more money flowing from the top line all the way to the bottom line as free cash flow.

EOG presented its free cash flow expectations for 2026 through 2028. As we can see, it's heavily dependent on the overall price of oil, with a higher WTI price leading to a higher FCF for shareholders. The $10 billion to $18 billion amounts are cumulative over the 3-year period of 2026-2028. During the prior 3-year period, 2023-2025, EOG saw $14.4 billion in free cash flow, which was entirely returned to shareholders via regular dividends, special dividends, and buybacks.

It's important to note a distinct difference in the view of what comprises free cash flow from CNQ to EOG. CNQ views free cash flow as anything after capital expenses and its regular dividends. EOG views free cash flow as any cash flow available after its capital expenses. This means that they view their dividends as coming from free cash flow and part of their free cash flow rewards to shareholders, unlike CNQ.

Cash flow is highly sensitive to oil prices. This is because EOG has a very limited hedging program and is more exposed to the absolute price changes of oil than some of its peers. EOG primarily hedges its natural gas production. With oil prices being well above their $55 or even $70 price targets, this means that we are directly going to see more free cash flow developed in the present than initially anticipated. However, EOG has continued to be extremely focused on returning all of its excess cash flow to its shareholders while continuing its production growth plans. This means that while oil prices remain extremely elevated during the Iran conflict, EOG is simply growing the basket of free cash flow that it can return to you and me as shareholders. EOG has made it very clear that their predominant focus in recent years is to return free cash flow in the form of share buybacks because they believe that their shares are undervalued.

We would agree that on a price-to-OCF basis, EOG is cheap relative to its past. If they continue to effectively buy back shares with their excess cash flow, we could see more OCF per share growth than initially anticipated. As well, it would allow them to reward shareholders with larger regular common dividends because the number of common shareholders would be reduced. To return the same amount of money as dividends to a smaller pool of shareholders means a larger per-share dividend.

We anticipate that much of the excitement around earnings will be management's discussion and analyst questions concerning the timeframe not covered in the initial earnings release. EOG is well-positioned to provide strong returns through this excess free cash flow because of its low breakeven point and commitment to return 100% of free cash flow returns to shareholders.

EOG reports earnings after the market closes on May 6.

Conclusion

EOG is cheap based on its price-to-OCF. They are set to be rewarded strongly from continued volatility within oil prices that we've seen over the last number of months. Every single day that oil prices remain higher than expected, and every single month that they remain elevated, provides EOG a greater and greater supply of free cash flow to benefit its shareholders. Considering that current prices are well above their expected amount for this year, as well as their high-end expectation for the next three years, this continues to increase the amount of free cash flow that EOG has at its disposal to reward its shareholders.

My personal preference would not be to see a rapid special dividend issue or to see a quick regular dividend hike because neither of those properly factors in the limited time frame that the current oil pricing volatility will have. Instead, I would prefer to see an increase in share buybacks to rapidly reduce the overall number of shares outstanding to enable EOG to reward shareholders over the long term, rather than a quick short-term win.

Our approach to investing is simple. We put emotions aside and focus on what data and facts can provide us. When we're looking at the fundamental health of a company, we recognize and understand that emotions and sentiment play a big role in many people's investing philosophies. Data wins in the end, especially over the long term. This is how we can rise above the chaos of emotions and find clarity. Clarity of perspective that we enjoy as Dividend Kings.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!
